title: Quantum Skyrmions in Mixed States of Light and their Nested Topology
authors: Amit Kam, Charles Roques-Carmes, Shai Tsesses, Aviv Karnieli
abstract: Topological quasiparticles of light, such as classical and quantum optical skyrmions, have so far relied on fully coherent or pure quantum states whose topology is encoded in the entanglement between polarization and two-dimensional spatial modes. Here we show that skyrmionic topology can emerge directly within the density matrix of a mixed quantum state. We introduce a framework in which a coherence-Stokes vector defines a topological texture over the density matrix, enabling the realization of quantum skyrmions using only a pseudospin and a real or synthetic one-dimensional space of modes. For a single photon, the density matrix is analogous to the coherence matrix of classical light, and can be encoded using partially coherent electromagnetic fields. We further analyze the topological texture encoded in a bipartite entangled photon pair, showing how skyrmions arise not only in the full bi-photon system, but also in its reduced subspaces with any pseudospin-mode combination simultaneously. We then explore the robustness of such skyrmions to environmental noise, and discover their persistence in mixed-quantum states of multiple photons. Finally, we propose a feasible experimental route to generate and measure such skyrmions using integrated photonic networks, and suggest avenues for similar implementations in other quantum systems. Our work paves the way for robustly encoding classical information on partially coherent light or on mixed quantum states and for encoding topological charges on many-body quantum systems.
summary: This paper explores how topological quasiparticles known as skyrmions can exist within mixed quantum states rather than just pure states. The authors show that these skyrmions can be 'nested,' meaning their topological properties persist across different parts of an entangled multi-photon system. They also demonstrate that these structures are remarkably robust against environmental noise like dephasing and depolarization. Finally, they propose a practical way to create and measure these states using integrated photonic circuits, which could lead to new methods for robust information encoding.
main_problem: Extending the realization of optical skyrmions from pure quantum states to mixed quantum states and investigating the robustness and 'nested' nature of topological charges in bipartite and multi-photon systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that skyrmionic topology can emerge in the density matrix of mixed states, can be realized in reduced 1D dimensions, and exhibits a 'nested' structure where topological charges persist across various reduced subspaces and are robust to environmental noise.
method: The study uses a mathematical framework involving a coherence-Stokes vector derived from the density matrix, a sparse representation technique for constructing valid PSD matrices, and numerical simulations of noise channels (dephasing, depolarization, and additive noise).
model_or_system: The physical systems include single-photon states with polarization and 1D spatial/synthetic modes, bipartite entangled photon pairs (Bell states), and N-photon multi-photon mixed states, with a proposed experimental implementation using integrated photonic MZI meshes.
key_observables: Topological charge (skyrmion number Q), coherence-Stokes vector texture, and the presence of line defects.
important_parameters: Number of eigenvectors (rank d), number of modes (M), dephasing strength, mixing probability, and relative phase (phi).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the ability to engineer specific density matrix elements and relies on the availability of programmable photonic networks for implementation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates skyrmions in 1D partially coherent light and the effect of rank on the skyrmion number; Figure 4 shows the experimental concept using MZI meshes and the topological switching of Q as a function of phase.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of skyrmions in mixed states, develops the mathematical framework for 1D and 2D textures, analyzes bipartite and multi-photon nested topologies, tests robustness against various noise models, and concludes with an experimental implementation proposal.
why_interesting: It is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it shows how topological protection can be maintained in noisy, mixed-state regimes and introduces a new way to encode information using the density matrix itself.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Skyrmions encoded in the partial coherence of light. (a) An optical skyrmion encoded in one-dimensional partially coherent light, in either real space or synthetic dimension (such as frequency). (b) The designed skyrmion number is shown as a function of the number of retained eigenvectors in the density matrix. In particular, when keeping d = |Q| + 1 eigenvectors, the reconstructed texture is guaranteed to faithfully reproduce the target skyrmion. (c) A density matrix is constructed from the analytic solution with eigenmodes defined in Eqs. 8. (d) From this density matrix, we compute the coherence Stokes vector and plot its associated skyrmion texture for M = 11 spatial modes.

Figure 2: Nested Topology of a two-photon entangled skyrmion encoded in the partial coherence of light. (a) Two photons, A and B, each possess a polarization σi and a spatial degree of freedom xi. The joint two-photon wavefunction, given in Eq. 10, is engineered to carry a topological charge Q = −1, yielding the skyrmion texture shown in the illustration. (b)-(c) The system has four reduced subspaces, formed by tracing different pairs of polarization and spatial-mode sectors, each revealing an emergent skyrmion texture: (b) two local skyrmions appearing in the reduced density matrices of photons A and B individually, and (c) two non-local (entangled) skyrmions in the hybrid subspace made up...

Figure 3: Robustness of local and non-local skyrmions. (a) Skyrmion number Q in local and nonlocal reduced subspaces (M = 80) as a function of the dephasing strength σ for a Gaussian phase- noise channel applied to the two-photon density matrix (Eq. 11). The skyrmion number of the local reduced subspace xB, σB (blue) remains invariant under two-photon dephasing, while the non-local reduced subspace (xB, σA) (orange) remains invariant up to σ ∼1.5, indicating the gradual destruction of the non-local skyrmion. The textures in panel (d) visualize this transition. (b) Topological charge Q for a non-local skyrmion as a function of the dephasing strength σ for a Gaussian phase-noise channel and...

Figure 4: Experimental concept for generating and tuning a two-photon skyrmionic state on chip. (a) A pair of entangled photons is prepared in a Bell state encoded in two paths and two polariza- tions. The two polarizations in each path are further separated into two spatial bins by a polarizing beam splitter (PBS) and converted to the same V polarization by a half-wavelength plate. Each of the paths is then incident upon a reconfigurable, integrated interferometric network which implements a controlled mapping from two incident spatial bins into two designed eigenmodes |u1⟩and |u2⟩.
